choices: Draw the boundary between two classes with the widest possible margin (gap)
Memorize every training point and vote among neighbors
Ask yes/no questions down a flowchart
Predict an open-ended number like a price
items.explanation: An SVM positions the dividing boundary so the empty margin on each side is as wide as possible. Voting among neighbors is k-NN and yes/no questions are a tree, so those describe other models, not an SVM.

items.prompt: In an SVM, what are the 'support vectors'?
choices: The labels printed on the graph's axes
The two farthest-apart points in the data
The borderline examples closest to the boundary that pin it in place
The yes/no questions the model asks
items.explanation: Support vectors are the critical borderline points nearest the boundary; they are what hold the dividing line in position and give the method its name. They are not axis labels, the most distant points, or questions — those belong to other ideas.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches an SVM's widest-margin boundary?
choices: Following a recipe's steps in order
Painting the fairest dividing line right down the middle of the widest empty street between two neighborhoods
Asking the five houses nearest you for their vote
Stacking many guesses and averaging them
items.explanation: An SVM seeks the line down the middle of the widest gap, just like the fairest dividing line in the widest part of an empty street. Asking the nearest houses is k-NN and averaging many guesses is an ensemble, so those images fit other models.

items.prompt: In plain words, what is the SVM 'kernel trick' for?
choices: It deletes the support vectors to save memory
It bends the space so a curvy boundary becomes a straight one that can be separated
It counts how many neighbors vote for each class
It makes the margin as small as possible
items.explanation: The kernel trick warps the space so that classes needing a curvy split can be separated by a straight boundary instead. It does not remove support vectors, tally neighbor votes, or shrink the margin — an SVM actually wants the margin wide.

items.prompt: Match the model to its description: 'classify a new point by a majority vote of its k closest examples.'
choices: Decision tree
Support Vector Machine
Random forest
k-Nearest Neighbors
items.explanation: Voting among the k closest examples is precisely k-Nearest Neighbors. A decision tree asks yes/no questions, an SVM draws a widest-margin line, and a random forest votes among many trees — none of which is the nearest-neighbor idea.

items.prompt: Why does scaling the features (putting them on comparable ranges) matter so much for k-NN?
choices: Because k-NN asks yes/no questions and needs clean labels
Because k-NN relies on distances, and a feature with a huge range would dominate the distance and drown out the others
Because k-NN trains for a very long time
Because k-NN draws a widest-margin boundary
items.explanation: k-NN decides by distance, so a feature measured in large units would swamp the distance calculation unless features are put on comparable scales. k-NN does not ask questions or draw margins, and it barely trains at all, so those reasons do not apply.

lessons.title: Ensembles: Wisdom of the Crowd
lessons.content:
We end the tour with the trick that often wins in practice: don't rely on one model — combine **many**.

**Hook.** Ask one person to guess the number of jellybeans in a jar and they'll be off. Average the guesses of a whole crowd and the answer is often startlingly close. That is the **wisdom of the crowd**, and in machine learning it is called an **ensemble**: combine many models so their individual mistakes tend to **cancel out**, leaving a more accurate whole.

**Build — Random Forest.** The most popular ensemble of decision trees is the **random forest**. Instead of growing one deep tree (which, recall, tends to overfit — memorize the training data), you grow **many** trees, each trained on a **random slice** of the data and features, so the trees end up different from one another. To predict, every tree gets a say and the forest takes a **majority vote**. Because the trees' errors are varied, voting cancels much of the noise: a random forest is usually **far more accurate and far less overfit than a single tree**. (Keep the names straight: one tree is *not* a forest — a forest is many trees combined by a vote.)

**Build — Boosting.** A different way to combine models is **boosting**. Here the models are built **in sequence**, and each new model focuses on fixing the **mistakes** the previous ones made — like a relay team where each member patches the last member's errors. The famous versions are **gradient boosting** and **XGBoost**. These boosted-tree methods win on a great deal of real-world **tabular** data (the rows-and-columns spreadsheets that run businesses).

**Build — No single best model.** A grounding truth ties it all together: there is **no single best model** for every problem. The practical recipe is to set a simple **baseline** (recall: a trivial predictor you must beat), **try several** of these models, **evaluate** them honestly on held-out data, and keep whatever works best. And remember — none of these are neural networks, yet these classical models still power **tons** of real-world tabular and business systems today.

**Recap.** An ensemble combines many models so their mistakes cancel — wisdom of the crowd. A random forest grows many varied trees and votes, beating a single overfit tree; boosting (gradient boosting, XGBoost) builds models in sequence, each fixing the last one's errors, and dominates much tabular data. There is no universally best model: baseline, try several, evaluate, and pick the winner.

items.prompt: What is an 'ensemble' in machine learning?
choices: A single very deep decision tree
Combining many models so their individual mistakes tend to cancel out
A model that keeps all the training data and votes among neighbors
A boundary drawn with the widest possible margin
items.explanation: An ensemble pools many models so their errors offset one another, giving a more accurate whole — the wisdom of the crowd. A single deep tree, a neighbor-voting store, and a widest-margin boundary are each just one model, not a combination.

items.prompt: What does a RANDOM FOREST do?
choices: Grows one deep tree and trusts it completely
Keeps every training point and compares distances
Grows many decision trees on random slices of the data, then takes a majority vote
Bends the space so a curvy boundary becomes straight
items.explanation: A random forest trains many varied trees on random slices of the data and features and combines them by majority vote. Trusting one deep tree, comparing distances, and bending space describe a single tree, k-NN, and an SVM kernel respectively.

items.prompt: Why is a random forest usually better than a single decision tree?
choices: A single tree is impossible to train
Voting across many varied trees cancels much of the noise, so the forest is more accurate and less overfit
A forest uses no data at all
A forest is just one tree drawn larger
items.explanation: Because the trees differ, their errors are varied and the vote averages much of the noise away, reducing overfitting and raising accuracy. A single tree can certainly be trained, and a forest is genuinely many trees, not one drawn bigger.

items.prompt: A learner claims 'a single decision tree is the same thing as a random forest.' Why is this WRONG?
choices: A forest is many different trees combined by a vote, not one tree
A forest is actually simpler than one tree
A forest contains no trees at all
A single tree always votes with itself many times
items.explanation: A random forest is a whole collection of distinct trees whose votes are combined, which is what makes it sturdier than any one tree. It is not a single tree, it is built from many trees, and it is not simpler than one tree.

items.prompt: What is the core idea of BOOSTING?
choices: Keep only the single best model and discard the rest
Train every model on the exact same data at once and average them blindly
Build models in sequence, each one fixing the previous models' mistakes
Remove the hardest examples so the model looks better
items.explanation: Boosting builds models one after another, with each new model concentrating on correcting the errors left by the earlier ones. It is sequential and error-correcting, unlike keeping a lone model or training everything in parallel with no error focus.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches BOOSTING?
choices: A jury all voting at the same moment
A relay team where each member patches the previous member's errors
A single expert deciding alone
Drawing the widest line down an empty street
items.explanation: Boosting works in sequence, each model fixing what the last got wrong, just like a relay where each runner patches the prior errors. Voting all at once is more like a forest, and the lone expert and widest-street images describe other methods.

items.prompt: Gradient boosting and XGBoost are especially strong on which kind of data?
choices: Real-world tabular (rows-and-columns) data
Only handwritten signatures
Only raw audio waveforms
No data at all
items.explanation: Boosted-tree methods like gradient boosting and XGBoost win on a great deal of tabular, spreadsheet-style data that runs businesses. They are not limited to signatures or audio, and like any model they need data to work.

items.prompt: Which statement reflects the 'no single best model' idea?
choices: Always use a random forest, no matter the problem
Set a baseline, try several models, evaluate them honestly on held-out data, and keep what works best
Pick the model with the longest name
Never test a model; just trust the first one you build
items.explanation: Because no one model wins everywhere, the sound practice is to baseline, try several, evaluate fairly on data the models have not seen, and keep the best. Always forcing one model, choosing by name length, or skipping evaluation all ignore that lesson.

items.prompt: How does the 'wisdom of the crowd' explain why ensembles help?
choices: One model is always right, so the crowd is unnecessary
Crowds make every model slower and worse
The crowd just copies the single best guess
Many models' mistakes tend to cancel out, so the combined answer is more reliable than any one model's
items.explanation: Like averaging many jellybean guesses, combining many models lets their varied errors offset, yielding a more reliable answer than any single model. A lone model is not always right, and a true ensemble combines independent guesses rather than copying one.

items.prompt: What is the key DIFFERENCE between a random forest and boosting?
choices: A forest builds many trees independently and votes; boosting builds models in sequence, each fixing the last one's errors
They are exactly the same method with two names
A forest uses no trees while boosting uses only trees
A forest works in sequence while boosting votes all at once
items.explanation: A random forest grows its trees independently and combines them by vote, whereas boosting builds models one after another to correct prior mistakes. The remaining options swap or merge these ideas, getting the independent-versus-sequential distinction backwards.

items.prompt: Even though none of these classical models are neural networks, why are they still worth knowing in 2026?
choices: They have been completely replaced and are never used
They still power tons of real-world tabular and business systems
They only work as toys and never in production
They can only be used after a neural network fails
items.explanation: These classical models remain workhorses behind a great deal of real tabular and business software, so they are very much in use. They are neither obsolete, toys, nor a mere fallback to neural networks.
